title: Squeezing and adiabaticity breaking in time-dependent quantum harmonic oscillators
authors: Mattia Orlandini, Beatrice Donelli, Lorenzo Buffoni, Stefano Gherardini
abstract: The quantum harmonic oscillator with time-dependent frequency is a paradigmatic model of driven quantum dynamics and one of the few nontrivial systems that admits an exact analytical solution. In this review paper, we present a unified treatment of the time-dependent oscillator based on the Lewis-Riesenfeld invariant method, Bogoliubov transformations and the Ermakov-Pinney equation. We show how these approaches naturally connect to squeezing for the description of excitations production, and to the breakdown of adiabaticity under generic frequency protocols. Exact results for sudden quenches and smooth ramps are discussed in detail. By explicitly bridging invariant methods and squeezing formalism, this review is meant to provide a comprehensive framework for understanding nonequilibrium dynamics in quadratic potentials, with applications ranging from thermodynamics and condensed matter to quantum control theory.
summary: This review paper provides a unified mathematical framework to describe the dynamics of quantum harmonic oscillators with time-varying frequencies. It proves that the production of non-adiabatic excitations is fundamentally equivalent to the generation of quantum squeezing. The work connects these dynamics to the Kibble-Zurek mechanism and provides exact solutions for various driving protocols.
main_problem: The paper seeks to provide a unified theoretical framework for understanding the nonequilibrium dynamics of quantum harmonic oscillators with time-dependent frequencies, specifically focusing on the link between adiabaticity breaking and the production of squeezing.
main_result: The authors establish that the dynamical transformation of a time-dependent QHO is exactly a squeezing operator and demonstrate that the breakdown of adiabaticity is directly linked to the generation of squeezing, with scaling laws for excitations following the Kibble-Zurek mechanism.
method: The study utilizes the Lewis-Riesenfeld invariant method, Bogoliubov transformations, and the Ermakov-Pinney equation to derive exact analytical solutions and relate them to squeezing formalism.
model_or_system: A quantum harmonic oscillator (QHO) with a time-dependent frequency omega(t) and potentially a time-dependent mass M(t).
key_observables: Squeezing parameter (r), adiabaticity factor (Q), position and momentum variances, transition amplitudes, and excitation probabilities.
important_parameters: Frequency protocols (sudden quenches, smooth ramps, linear symmetric quenches), adiabaticity factor Q, squeezing modulus r, and the quench rate delta.
main_assumptions: The invariant is assumed to be Hermitian and without time derivatives; the spectrum is assumed to be discrete; the initial invariant is proportional to the initial Hamiltonian.
figures_summary: Figure 1 shows a negative binomial distribution of excitations; Figure 2 illustrates the correlation between adiabaticity and squeezing; Figure 4 shows the time evolution of frequency, adiabaticity, and squeezing for non-linear quenches; Figure 5 shows the evolution of the squeezing parameter.
paper_structure: The paper begins with a unified treatment of the QHO using Lewis-Riesenfeld invariants and the Ermakov-Pinney equation, moves into the connection between Bogoliubov transformations and squeezing, analyzes specific frequency protocols (quenches and ramps), and concludes with applications to the Kibble-Zurek mechanism and scaling in many-body systems.
why_interesting: It provides a comprehensive bridge between mathematical invariant methods and the physical intuition of squeezing, which is highly relevant for understanding non-equilibrium dynamics, quantum control, and phase transitions in many-body systems like the Dicke or Bose-Hubbard models.
analysis_basis: full PDF text, analyzed in chunks

figures.caption: FIG. 1: Negative binomial distribution as a function of k, for ˜p = 10−1 (blue histogram), 10−2 (red histogram) and 10−4 (yellow histogram).

FIG. 2: Time evolution of the QHO under an hyperbolic quench with parameters ωi = 1, ωf = 3, τ = 0 (in arbitrary units with ℏ= M = 1). (a) Frequency protocol ω(t) as a function of time for ϵ = 0.001, 0.25, 0.5, 1, 2 [see Eq. (152)]. (b) Adiabaticity factor Q(t) [Eq. (145)] as a function of time. (c) Modulus of the squeezing parameter r(t) as a function of time. In panels (b) and (c), the values of ϵ are the same as those used in panel (a).

FIG. 3: Position and momentum variances for a sudden quench protocol as functions of time for n = 0 (blue solid lines), together with their values averaged over one period (black dashed line), as defined in Eq. (172) and Eq. (173), respectively. The pre- and post-quench frequencies are set to ωi = 1 and ωf = 3 (in arbitrary units with ℏ= M = 1).

FIG. 4: Time evolution of (a) the squared frequency protocol ω2(t), (b) the adiabaticity factor Q(t), and (c) the squeezing parameter r(t) for the non-linear quench defined in Eq. (194). Different colors correspond to different values of η = {1, 1.5, 2, 2.5, 3}, as indicated in the legend. The asymptotic values of r(t) and Q(t), predicted by Eq. (195), are shown by the dashed lines. The breakdown of adiabaticity is signaled by the finite asymptotic values attained by r(t) and Q(t) −1 at long times, in agreement with the prediction of Eq. (195).

FIG. 5: Time evolution of (a) the squeezing parameter r(t) and (b) the adiabaticity factor Q(t) as a func- tion of the rescaled time t/τ for a linear quench protocol. Different colors correspond to different values of τ = {12.5, 25, 50, 100, 200}, as indicated in the legend (in arbitrary units with ℏ= M = 1). The black dashed line represents the theoretical prediction of Eq. (193). Independent of the protocol duration, the squeezing parameter r(t) saturates to the non-zero value predicted by Eq. (193).
